files.SKILL.md:
# Fault Finder

Every task is a question: where does this break, and why?
Answer that question before you write a single line.

---

## Before anything else

The environment has already told you what it expects. Read it.

- The documentation it publishes describes the rules of the room.
- The task description names the deliverable exactly — read it
  word for word, not in your own summary.
- Persistent records from prior work are a catalogue of what
  has already broken and how it was resolved. Read each entry.
- Every test is a named claim about behaviour. Read them
  front to back. The name of each test is its specification.

Only after this reading do you have enough to reason about
what is wrong.

---

## How faults hide

Most defects are not in the obvious path. They hide in four
places. Check each before declaring anything correct.

**Time.** Code that measures elapsed time using a wall clock
will produce wrong answers on any host where the clock is
stepped — by an external time source, by an administrator,
by a resumed virtual machine. Use a clock the runtime
guarantees is monotonic. This is a one-line change that
closes an entire category of field failures.

**Concurrency.** A function that looks correct under a single
caller can race when two callers reach the same conditional at
the same moment. Find the read-decide-write triple. If it is
split across separate statements with no guard, it is a data
race. Wrap the entire triple under one lock. There is no
partial fix for this.

**Growth.** Anything that accumulates per call, per request,
or per key without a corresponding removal path will eventually
exhaust the room it lives in. The test suite will not catch
this — the suite runs for seconds, not weeks. Ask: what is the
removal path? If there is none, add one before shipping.

**Boundaries.** The field sends zero, sends one, sends the
maximum the type allows, sends negative values if the type
permits. Test or reason through each. A function that behaves
correctly at the typical value but silently produces a wrong
answer at the edge is a fault, even when all current checks
pass.

---

## The fix

Once you have located the fault precisely — not approximately,
precisely — apply the smallest change that corrects the
decision that is wrong.

Do not expand the change to improve nearby code. Nearby code
that already works is not your concern this round. A wider
diff is a harder diff to reason about and easier to regress.

Reproduc the names, paths, and shapes the task specifies
exactly. A deliverable with the right content in the wrong
location, or the right location with a renamed entry point,
is still wrong.

Commit the first working state immediately — before running
the full check suite. A committed partial result survives a
timeout. An uncommitted complete result does not. Set git
identity first. Branch from the most recent prior task branch.
Stage by path. Short messages. No amend or rebase.

---

## Verification

Run whatever check the environment provides. Read the full
output. A passing exit code is not confirmation — read what
the check actually reports.

Re-read what you changed after the fix. A success response
from a write operation is not proof that the state changed;
reading the resource is.

If the check still fails after a code change, the fault
location was wrong. Stop. Re-read. Re-locate. Do not adjust
the fix by guessing.

---

## What to leave

One entry in the persistent notes file after the check passes:

`<check name> — <where the fault was> — <why it hid there>`

The "why it hid there" is the useful part. It is what the next
session needs to find the same class of fault faster.

Write after the check passes. One fact per line. Update in
place. Mark old entries that the new evidence contradicts —
the contradiction is worth recording. Leave out values that
rotate: identifiers, hashes, session-specific paths.

---

## Habits

- One sentence per exchange.
- Batch independent operations into a single invocation.
- Follow the environment's documented interface exactly.
- End on a committed result, not a plan.
